comments.title: The Mgical Battle of Gamergate?
comments.content:
You might recall my previous questions about the unfolding Gamergate 2. My perspective is very limited, since I don’t play videogames. However, I know a small number of “alternative game” players – Helldivers 2 and Escape from Tarkov – and I’m partway into The Magical Battle of Britain, so I’ve been wondering if Dion Fortune’s methods could be used by the games’ players to monkey wrench the machination-ery of the peculiarly anti-gamer game designers. The gamer I was talking with filters my Druidry through his garden, apiary and love of good ol’ D&D, so he understood what I was getting at, but I’m not fluent enough in videogame lore to suggest appropriate symbols or ritual.
Do you think protecting Greater Earth and Tarkov using a Fortune’s methods might work? Is there much risk or even a chance of success? And (if possible) – might you and the commentariat be persuaded to give a few pointers on symbol selection and ritual design?
Many thanks!
Rhydlyd

replies.title: Re: The Mgical Battle of Gamergate?
replies.content: That's an odd idea but not an impossible one. You'd want to work with images from within the games, certainly. As for ritual design, it depends very much on what the level of occult knowledge you can expect from participants -- something very simple might be wisest if most people don't have much background. And remember -- keep it secret! If you publish the details, they can be monkeywrenched.

replies.title: Re: The Mgical Battle of Gamergate?
replies.content:
Wait, wait... I'm out of my depth and into academic questions because I'm not going to do anything since I have neither dog nor fight here... but... how do you keep a public ritual secret?
Or... maybe... what stays secret and what gets published to potential participants?
And... why did I even think to ask that?
Clearly, I'm barking up a forest of wrong trees without a paddle or a canoe...
... any chance I could get a nickel tour of the mechanics of a simple working?
R

replies.title: Re: The Mgical Battle of Gamergate?
replies.content:
1) You can't keep a public ritual secret. That's why public rituals generally fail. The core elements of the ritual -- the specific images and concepts on which the participants concentrate, and the intention behind those -- should always be kept private. Furthermore, you can give some details out to a large outer circle of participants and keep others private within an inner circle.

2) Fortune gives those quite clearly in the book. The working she led, building up certain specific images in meditation, is a very effective method for large groups. So you simply have to decide on an intention -- the goal of the working -- which should be as simple and precise as possible, and then choose appropriate images and concepts for participants to concentrate on in regular (weekly at least, if possible daily) meditations.

replies.title: Re: The Mgical Battle of Gamergate?
replies.content:
Thank you, that's what I thought before I tripped myself up. (Pesky thing, that ego.) Anyway - My divination on participating myself was unfavorable, so back to studies as usual, but I will mention your advice to my friend.
R

replies.title: Re: The Mgical Battle of Gamergate?
replies.content: I'm not a gamer, so I can't imagine "anti-gamer game designers" staying in business. Sounds to me like selling fishermen an accessory guaranteed to make your boat sink. What am I missing?

replies.title: Re: The Mgical Battle of Gamergate?
replies.content: Ideology. A great many people in the gaming business, as in other fields that profit off creativity, believe that it's their life's mission to ram woke ideology down the throats of everyone in the world, and despise those people who haven't bought into their belief system. There's a great deal of that in the publishing world, too, which is why you won't see my novels coming from any of the big corporate publishers.

replies.title: Re: The Mgical Battle of Gamergate?
replies.content: Oh, this makes sense. I've seen it in other fields. Thanks.

replies.title: Re: The Mgical Battle of Gamergate?
replies.content:
It seems like there's a lot of potential here for unpredicted forms of semiotic undertow in the symbolic material one would be working with here, from multiple directions. As the famous line a gamer might recognize goes: "prepare for unforeseen consequences".

The most important reason to be on guard about unforeseen consequences is that the previous series by the creators of Helldivers is Magicka. Helldivers shares a lot of its emergent-black-comedy gameplay aesthetic with Magicka. And Magicka is (a) specifically about magic, and (b) to a significant extent specifically a backdrop to a farce about magic blowing up in your face because you weren't careful, and (c) to a significant extent specifically about you and your allies' magic farcically blowing up in each others' faces (especially when you evoke opposing elements against the same target at point blank range), and (d) to a significant extent, on both the plot level and the mechanics level, about chaos and destruction resulting from actions improvised in haste, failures of communication, and comedically careless disregard for life. Also, in both series, death is more of a comedy thing: in Magicka, living player characters can trivially resurrect others with just two single-keystroke elemental evocations, and in one chapter they get stuck in Niflheim and try to beat up Death; in Helldivers, Super Earth recruits a constant stream of reinforcements, and the dying and dead are little noticed or remembered. If these associations with death got enough spillover significance invested in them, it's unclear to what extents they would transpose over to this world in a favorable "the danger of death is trivial" way, versus an unfavorable "people trivialize the danger of death" way.

Within the series Helldivers itself, Super Earth is written as a farcically dystopian authoritarian police state polity, both in culture and in government. So a magical intention to uphold Super Earth might get translated to upholding, not just its military-positive or localism-positive attitudes, but its totalitarianism, or its "managed democracy" shenanigans and propaganda (which are more characteristic of how the cultural bloc that lined up on the anti-Gamergate side holds power). Or maybe it would translate to upholding the satirical discreditation of the symbols "democracy" and "liberty", without upholding the part about democracy and liberty being parts of an ideal. Or maybe vice versa. Maybe it would translate to upholding Super Earth's propagandized callousness toward the enemy factions and the disbelief in their valid grievances.

I don't know as much about Escape from Tarkov, but it looks like the major conflict is between the private military contractors hired by a consortium of international capital interests that provoked a war in a Russian special economic zone to enable their mysterious plans involving illegal laboratories and military espionage, and the private military contractors hired by the Russian government to enable investigations and prevent exfiltration of ill-gotten gains. Also the developers are from Russia. A magical intention to symbolically uphold and defend the war-torn city of Tarkov (did you relay that detail correctly?) might spill over into powering or entrenching some parts of the framings of real-world conflicts involving Russia.

With all the different layers of irony, subversion, conflict, and potential hypocritical projection going on here, it seems like it would be hard to pick any small set of symbols and try to empower or uphold them without potentially having several other unintended effects at the same time.

replies.title: Re: The Mgical Battle of Gamergate?
replies.content:
OP - Yikes. That explains the "don't you dare" divination I got.
And thank you for the background.
While this is a really interesting story in process, I'm going back to the corner of Middle and Nowhere where I can read about current events in a few years when the histories hit bookshop.org.
And many thanks to our host for allowing me to find a lesson without clobbering myself.

replies.title: Re: The Mgical Battle of Gamergate?
replies.content:
Assuming that a magical project was in fact in proper continuity with the original Gamergate controversy, it doesn't seem impossible that this would be the sort of chaotic situation that might work out okay overall while the practitioner had Eris feeling favorable toward them, rather than feeling put out. One possible starting point to improving the odds there would be to really meditate on Eris's implied complaint (and/or her implied form of "malicious" compliance with human choices relating to conflict) constituted by the original Gamergate controversy. Still, for most people who might attempt such a work, even if they were to start preparing themselves with such a practice of reflection, there might not really be a way for them to clean up their act enough in time to make the project viable.

And even assuming that they did, if someone's real goal was to bring benefit to a collectivity that wasn't itself also going to be cleaning up its act, that also might not work.

comments.title: Review of "He Is Frenzy" and Occult "Capital" Question
comments.content:
Good Evening,

I missed wishing a pleasant Alban Eiler/Spring Equinox to everyone last week, so I please accept belated well wishes. For anyone who observes Good Friday, may it be spiritually fulfilling and observed in the appropriate mood (I hear that different denominations differ on whether it should be somber or celebratory).

To Share: I put together my fairly brief thoughts on Galina Krasskova's He Is Frenzy, a book on various devotional practices and experiences for Odin, here: https://jpowellrussell.com/#book_he_is_frenzy

To Ask: A quick one today: in some podcasts and comments, you've talked about how in the past, different cities in America have been the "occult capital" at different times - Boston, Chicago, New York, LA. I'm curious, is there one now, and if so, where is it? Nothing immediately jumps to mind.

As always, thanks very much to JMG and everyone else here for all that you do.

My blessings to all who welcome them,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Review of "He Is Frenzy" and Occult "Capital" Question
replies.content: To the best of my knowledge, we're between occult capitals. The occult scene in the US right now is very diffuse, with groups and teachers scattered all anyhow across the country; we'll have to wait to see if and when (and where!) a new center will emerge.

replies.title: Re: Review of "He Is Frenzy" and Occult "Capital" Question
replies.content:
We Roman Catholics fast, go to Mass, and cover, take down, or turn to the wall all religious art. It’ll be back to normal on Easter Sunday.

What’s everybody else do?

—Princess Cutekitten

replies.title: Re: Review of "He Is Frenzy" and Occult "Capital" Question
replies.content: I'm not sure in much detail, as I've only ever been to Methodist and Episcopalian Easter services (and I don't know if I ever went to anything for Good Friday, maybe when I was a kid), but I was listening to a podcast with an Orthodox Christian, and he was talking about how the feeling is much more triumphant - Jesus is down in Hell kicking Satan's butt on his way back to give us the good news, so let's feel good about that.

replies.title: Re: Review of "He Is Frenzy" and Occult "Capital" Question
replies.content:
Blessings received and also asked for you, Jeff.

Good Friday symbolizes that when all seemed lost beyond hope, actually ultimate success was just about to be brought by God, conquering the pit of despair and evil, replacing destruction with full triumph. I can see how either sombre or celebratory could fit with different parts of this story. By Easter, completely joyous jubilation is the natural response to the story. But when exactly is the turning point? I can see that as open to interpretation.

I don't know anything about the worship of Odin, but I really liked your discussion of squeamishness. I'm the same way. For example, I admire that there is a lot of artistry in many terrifying horror stories and shocking movies. But I don't mind that I've never watched Alien or Saw, or read all of Poe or King, and I never will. I had a hard time with ritual murder and self-mortifying priest described in the Davinci Code novel (I didn't see the film).

I wonder if the occult capital is temporarily distributed cyberspace, with no physical location. If JMG's right about Internet collapse, eventually it would re-emerge as a physical center of energy.

Christopher from California
Mocha Amphibious Moose

replies.title: Re: Review of "He Is Frenzy" and Occult "Capital" Question
replies.content:
Thank you for the blessings and the kind words!

That makes good sense about the transition from sadness, to triumph, to jubilation, and I can see how different traditions would have settled on different emphases and lengths of time for each.

Yeah, I usually do fine with horror, though I had to acquire the taste in high school, and sometimes it bothers me more than others, but I'm very calm around actual accidental injuries, so the discussion of ordeals in the book was right at a very interesting nexus of concepts and feelings (also, to be fair, the descriptions of the physically harmful ordeals were not particularly graphic).

Hmm, interesting thought on the occult "center" being decentralized these days, with maybe a change coming. I wonder if it will arise in the same general area JMG posits as the likely cradle of a future North American Great Culture, or if it's still far too soon for that.

Cheers,
Jeff

There were a number of specific and detailed steps in the procedure whose full logic wasn't apparent or directly explained, written in such a way as to suggest that that the author believed themselves to have some sort of side source of confidence that these details were important as part of an overall design that worked with the other details, and with the details of human psychology, to produce a beneficial effect. I myself didn't know enough about ritual design to understand what they were doing well enough to memorize the suggested procedure, but I think it had recognizable opening and closing segments, and one step where people were supposed to mention emotions they were having about things, seemingly as a way to ground them out a bit.

comments.title: The Practice of the Violet Flame
comments.content:
I want to resume the practice of the Violet Flame with its decrees, which I took up for some time but then stopped. I wish to enjoy the benefits that are claimed for it, which I think are real.

I believe in the spiritual world, but I don’t believe in the immortality of Saint Germain, and the notion of ascended masters reminds me of Madam Blavatsky, of whom I am not a devotee.

Is it possible to benefit from a practice while being indifferent to, or disbelieving in, some of the core precepts endorsed by its most vocal exponents?

replies.title: Re: The Practice of the Violet Flame
replies.content:
Good heavens, yes. There's another name for "precepts" -- opinions. That's all they are. The practice is quite another matter. You might consider seeing if it makes sense to you to treat the more colorful opinions held by practitioners as symbolic rather than literally true -- or you can simply disregard that material altogether, and pursue the work.

The work is what counts. Make good use of it, and you can let the universe teach you what's true.

replies.title: Re: The Practice of the Violet Flame
replies.content:
Thanks. I’ll resume the practice. I can groove with beings like Boddhisatvas and helper angels rather than Koot Hoomi and World Teachers.

One factor that caused me to pause is my thoughts about the color of the Flame. Why violet, forsooth? In Golden Dawn materials the color of the divine light is golden or pure white.

But actually I’ve been reminded about that. One explanation goes that violet is the highest frequency color on the spectrum, the color nearest to ascending beyond the physical and into the higher planes.

Another occurs to me from the writings of Neville Goddard. He says that when prayers are really rocking the practitioner will be aware of a bluish flame-like light which Goddard compares with that of a British dessert flambé. It’s not exactly violet, but I fancy it must involve more or less the same principle.

replies.title: Re: The Practice of the Violet Flame
replies.content: The color of the flame is based on the theory of the seven rays, found all through old occult literature. The violet ray -- the 7th ray in the ascended master teachings -- is the ray of magic, because it unites the red ray of power with the blue ray of compassionate wisdom. Thus it's best suited for the practice of decrees, which require both power and wisdom.

replies.title: Re: The Practice of the Violet Flame
replies.content:
That is helpful to me.

As a child I loved the color red, especially an intense scarlet. When I became a painter I learned to appreciate the color blue, and to this day find it restful and peaceful. I love cerulean and ultramarine skies.

My horoscope is filled with fire. Mars is afflicted. I sometimes have outbreaks of terrible temper, seething with frustration. I suppose I should probably invoke Jupiter most days of the week, and maybe Venus too.

So violet unites the two. I guess I need wisdom. (Who doesn’t? Duh.) One thing I have experienced throughout my adult life is a certain powerlessness, a lack of agency, especially relating to money, which I fancy is probably the material expression of something going on with me higher up the planes. Maybe I need to get my red back, but with balance.

Thanks for your wisdom.

replies.title: Re: The Practice of the Violet Flame
replies.content:
If the St. Germaine story is true, he was an ordinary man who learned how to connect with a spiritual energy that already existed.
If his story is a myth, it represents our potential as ordinary people to connect with this spiritual energy.

In the last part of Neville's career, he taught what he called the Promise. He had a few very specific spiritual or visionary experiences. He said they fulfilled Biblical prophecy that applies to all believers. He also said eventually, in this life or another, everyone would have exactly that same sequence of experiences to complete their spiritual awakening. He was rather dogmatic about this. He was warned it could cost him his audience.

I saw someone mention that at the end of his life, he said, actually everyone's awakening will happen in its own way. But I haven't been able to track down if he really did make that disclaimer.

If the blue flame was from his Promise era, you could decide if it was only his own experience, or one you could expect to share.

Christopher from California
Mocha Amphibious Moose

replies.title: Re: The Practice of the Violet Flame
replies.content: Would the same reasoning go for participating in religious practice? I have been attending the mass at a local Roman Catholic church and the effects are undeniably powerful. But I can't go into this 100% - I cannot agree with all the doctrine,  nor can I give up my magic practice or my worship of other, older gods. Any advice?

replies.title: Re: The Practice of the Violet Flame
replies.content: Speaking as a practicing, very heterodox Roman Catholic, I can say that the same reasoning seems to hold to religious practice as well.  I attend the Mass, go to confession a few times a year, etc., all the while retaining my devotion to Mithras, Anat, and a few other gods and spirits (e.g., Santa Muerte, Jesus Malverde, etc.) and working my way through the lessons of the Mayan Order and the Rosicrucian Fellowship.  My experiences have led me to conclude that the gods, even the ones worshipped by traditional monotheists, just aren't that concerned with orthodoxy.  I've noticed no difference in the effectiveness of the Catholic sacraments, despite my heterodox beliefs and practices (though I maintain a nominal distinction between latria and dulia). In fact, once I took up the worship of Santa Muerte, I noticed that I got more grace, if you will, from the sacrament of confession.  I mostly have to make use of the Novus Ordo rites, and until I turned to Santa Muerte, confession with the local NO priest was almost useless in its effect.

replies.title: Re: The Practice of the Violet Flame
replies.content: The entire French magical tradition from the Middle Ages on assumed that you could keep on attending Mass even though your beliefs might be drastically different from those of the priest and the hierarchy, so I think you're on safe ground.

replies.title: Re: The Practice of the Violet Flame
replies.content: Another interesting example might be the Valentinians.  I've read quite a few of their surviving writings, and some of the scholarly introductions to those texts mention that many Valentinians attended the same worship services as the proto-orthodox.  They met privately for their teachings and practices, which they seem to have viewed as the higher, esoteric truth/versions of the exoteric practices/beliefs of the proto-orthodox.  One could argue, successfully in my opinion, that having a small, esoterically minded group of Christians within the larger, "mainstream" Church is the norm.

replies.title: Re: The Practice of the Violet Flame
replies.content:
This whole thread is very helpful to me since it matches my situation. I am Catholic in the same sense as people like Levi or Paladan. It took a lot of time, meditation and prayer to get to a point I could relax about that and accept it.

I find it helpful now to think of the literalist Catholic beliefs as being essentially another myth, and it is one that works well for some people. I can relate to it as a Myth.

The best of the literalist Catholics I know, especially older ones and especially older women, have a certain sweetness and simplicity to them that is quite beautiful, and I can stand to learn from them. I don't think of my faith the same way they do, but it is good to pray with them.

replies.title: Re: The Practice of the Violet Flame
replies.content:
That sounds pretty similar to how things played out for me, and how I view things now. I was a monotheist and fairly committed traditionalist Catholic for a while, then made my way into polytheism and veering off onto several different paths before coming (almost) full circle back into the Church as the heterodox polytheist that I now am. I'm now quite content, and enjoy quite a bit of peace of mind now.

I've found it very helpful to view religious doctrine and narratives (Catholic or not) as Myth and Archetype. Carl Jung has been a very helpful guide toward that end.

replies.title: Re: The Practice of the Violet Flame
replies.content:
“This whole thread is very helpful to me since it matches my situation.”

It gratifies me to read that. I started this thread, and it really pleases me when what I thought might be a dumb question turns out to generate answers that are helpful to other people.

I notice a lot of Catholics responding. There must be something going on with that.

comments.content:
Hey, just wanted to share an experiment in divination I'm trying with some success. I put together two divination methods for daily readings. I begin my morning first with a geomantic house reading, and two hours later do an Ogham reading. I started this after struggling to learn both divination systems successfully and thought doing *both* daily would help. I know learning two at once is not recommended, but circumstances led to me taking up both.

What's curious is that the readings can describe different situations during the day. The geomantic house reading shows what will happen or I can make happen in each house/area of my life, and my Ogham three-card reading can indicate more general matters or comment on another trend during the day. Sometimes the readings overlap in meaning, sometimes they forecast different aspects of my day. It has been interesting to learn from the results over time.

For questions:

1. For Magic Monday readers and not JMG, as JMG doesn't do dream analysis: how do you journal about your dreams? Do you have a journal template for tracking repeat dream imagery and themes over time?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It's well worth doing this, especially if you keep records of your divinations and review them regularly.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I haven't dream journaled in quite some time, but when I did, it was mostly just "write what I remember as soon as I can so I don't forget it," without much organization. Keeping a notebook by the bed to be written in if I woke up from a dream was essential for this. In Creative Dreaming, Patricia Garfield describes a method to make it possible to write in the dark while lying down to minimize how much you have to come out of the dream state to write. Basically, you put one finger on the top of the notebook to keep your line relatively straight and train your hand to go down a set space for a new line. Having a space pen or a pencil helps too.

If I were to take a more organized approach to imagery and themes, I might consider using a Zettelkasten approach (which I do for my other occult work). Scott Scheper's Antinet Zettelkasten is my preferred source on an analog zettelkasten set up, though you can find much of what he explains in the book via his website and youtube videos. One of his students also published a short little book called Soul Cards, which gives some specific tips for adapting a zettelkasten to journaling for personal development, so it might have some good insights here: https://www.iamhaileyrene.com/book/

Hope these help,
Jeff

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The two approaches to daily divination is a very helpful distinction to me. Thank you for that.

I like having a one-touch voice recorder (no need to look at a screen to turn it on) for drowsy notes. When awake, the audio can be transcribed.

There are many note taking programs where you can tag entries by date and see them in a journal form, and you can also tag entries by topic and click to see everything on the same theme. Many of these programs are free, so you can play around with no cost but some time. A quick search for free note taking software will show recommendations like Zim Wiki, Microsoft One Note, Joplin, Cherry Tree, and more. You can keep the file private on your own machine, or put it on a cloud for shared access from phone and devices, as you like.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I've kept a dream journal both on paper and digitally where I just wrote down all I could remember as soon as waking. I've also recorded voice notes. The best practice for me has been having dreamer friends, people who are also active dreamers and believe those dreams significant, with whom to share. Over time, I've adapted to letting go of many dreams, yet I'll often wake and feel something was significant, and in my head I recall and recount to the person and can hold until I write or call them. I've read about indigenous cultures that dream-shared upon waking, and thought it'd be lovely to do a spiritual retreat and incorporate bunking with others and sharing dreams upon waking.

Another benefit of sharing with others is that as you share, their questioning will draw out more detail. I like the practice of listening to someone's dream and when they blank on something, you fill it in - obviously erroneously 99% of the time - but that error can trigger them to remember. For example:
Dreamer: "I dreamt I was driving with a woman... it was..."
Friend: "Your mom?"
Dreamer: "What - no! Oh yeah, it was my girlfriend from high school."

Wishing you fruitful dream-time.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
This is why it's important to get competent at one or more systems of divination. Planetary debilities are complicated, since the planet is never just affected by one thing at a time -- Mercury will be retrograde, sure, but there's a whole solar system of other forces affecting him. If you know astrology, you can track those influences on a day by day basis and choose a time when the retrograde is outweighed by other factors; if you do some other kind of divination, you can use that for the same purpose.

Since the planets come between us and the signs, it's a little risky to suggest that the planets don't matter in sign magic; if you were out there among the stars, maybe, but the influence of the signs has to pass through the solar system to get to you.

The general point, though, is that people are superstitious about Mercury retrogrades. You can do magic quite effectively during a Mercury retrograde; you just have to take the condition of Mercury into account. Since Virgo is co-ruled by the dwarf planet Ceres, in addition, you can annoy the traditionalists no end by doing Virgo magic when Ceres is angular and strong by aspect, irrespective of Mercury's condition.

comments.title: The ego
comments.content:
Morning John,
I hope you are well. have a question I’ve been intending to ask for a while. I have suggested it as a post in the past but you haven’t done one and besides, I’m not clear as to whether it justifies it.
What is the role or purpose of the “ego” in the individuality’s journey from abred to Gwynfydd?
In many eastern traditions, as I understand it, the aim is to get rid or overcome ego. But it must have a role in the journey in some form and it would be useful to have a better understanding of what that is. My guess up till now is that it is to enable us to achieve a sense of “self” as opposed to being a part of an undifferentiated “whole”. But I may be wrong.
Kind regards
Averagejoe

replies.title: Re: The ego
replies.content:
This notion that the ego has to be gotten rid of is standard Piscean age thinking -- it's always about identifying something in the self that's the source of all the badness, and then getting rid of it! It doesn't work, of course, and after 2160 years you'd think that would be fairly clear.

The ego is simply your concept of yourself -- your representation of yourself, to use a more technical phrase. It becomes a problem if you mistake it for yourself, get defensive about it, and put effort into keeping yourself from noticing where the concept differs from the reality, by projecting the shadow and other unhelpful maneuvers. Recognize your ego as an incomplete and somewhat inaccurate reflection of who you are, and allow it to change and grow as you proceed in the work of self-knowledge, and it's a useful tool. Eventually, it becomes an accurate reflection of yourself, and then it's no problem at all.

replies.title: Re: The ego
replies.content:
Thanks John,
That is very helpful. But can you clarify what you mean by “projecting the shadow”. I think you must be referring to a Jungian concept, but I’ve not read much of his work.
Kind regards Averagejoe

comments.content:

replies.title: Re: Mundane astrology of upcoming planetary movements
replies.content:
This is a complex issue, because much of the writing you've mentioned depends on a technique -- interpreting the ingresses of outer planets into new signs -- that isn't traditional in mundane astrology. I think it's potentially a useful technique, but it hasn't had anything like enough experimentation and testing to bear the weight that some astrologers put on it. It's one thing to use such a method retrospectively, to interpret the past -- it's quite another to use it to make valid predictions about the future, where you don't have the events already laid out in front of you.

The conjunctions of the outer planets are another matter -- those have been used since the dawn of mundane astrology millennia ago. We passed through the beginning of a new era in 2020 with the Jupiter-Saturn conjunction of that year, a grand mutation (that is, the conjunction moved into a new element). I discussed that here:

https://www.ecosophia.net/the-grand-mutation-an-astrological-interlude/

The imminent Jupiter-Uranus conjunction and the Saturn-Neptune conjunction at 0° Aries in 2026 are also important, and I'll be publishing delineations of those on my Patreon and SubscribeStar venues -- I may make them both open posts, to interest new subscribers, and if so, I'll announce that on this journal.

One thing to keep in mind, though, is that the 2020 grand mutation had Uranus, the planet of technology, retrograde and in his fall. That means that the 199-year period until the next Grand Mutation will be a period of technological regression and breakdown, not a period of progress. As I noted in the essay linked above, the modern mythology of inevitable progress was defined by the Moon's position in the grand mutation of 1842, and so the era of progress ended in 2020; what we have now is the usual hangover of obsolete expectations.

replies.title: Re: Mundane astrology of upcoming planetary movements
replies.content: In my personal experience, the outer planets don't have rulership or weakness, but I know you disagree.

replies.title: Re: Mundane astrology of upcoming planetary movements
replies.content: I do indeed disagree. In my mundane charting, I find it very important to attend to the rulership, detriment, exaltation, and fall of Uranus and Neptune just as one would for any other planet. For that matter, even dwarf planets such as Ceres and Pluto have recognizable dignities -- astrologers in the early 1800s worked out those for Ceres, for example.

replies.title: Re: Mundane astrology of upcoming planetary movements
replies.content:
Not JMG, but I have been thinking heavily about the Saturn-Neptune conjunctions, and it has nothing to do with technological developments (I swear, they seem to think that the mere existence of Uranus and Aquarius inevitably means "technology").

Historically, S-N conjunctions seem to denote overall shifts in the geopolitical tenor - or, if you will, they denote "chapters" of history. The 1846 conjunction in Aquarius, at approximately the time Neptune was discovered, really "inaugurated" the Industrial Revolution as well as the political-social revolutions of the late 1840s and the 1860s. Following it was an S-N conjunction in Taurus in 1882 that brought about the Gilded Age with its emphasis on banking, colonies (Taurus rules land), and monopolies; then one in Leo in 1917 that brought about an emphasis on world leaders such as FDR, Churchill, Hitler, Stalin, and Hirohito (notice how much more readily those names resonate even today than their equivalents from, say, World War I). That era concluded in 1953 with a S-N conjunction in Libra, where the world seemed to "hang in the balance" between two superpowers, followed by the 1989 conjunction in Capricorn where one of the superpowers was removed from the game and "Big Everything" took over (note that Big Tech's influence likely comes from the involvement of Uranus in this conjunction).

So what seems likely is that we'll be somehow "closing" a Capricorn-themed chapter of history and "opening" an Aries-themed chapter. The last such chapter ran from 1703 to 1738, a period of history about which I am unfortunately woefully ignorant. Looking at it on Wikipedia, I do see a lot of wars, but then again, I see a lot of wars in several periods.

That said, my prediction is that the S-N in Aries heralds the end of the current US (and thus Western) hegemony, for better or for worse. The cracks in it are already starting to show. Wars and "interventions" (i.e., wars) are likely and the tenor of this Mars-ruled period is likely to be more martial overall than the previous, Saturn-ruled one was. The end of Western hegemony is likely to also mean the obsolescence and/or replacement of a number of international institutions, likely including the entire framework of international law. Nations will be increasingly "on their own" (Aries = independence). Separatist movements are likely to increase. I wouldn't be surprised to see a significant volcanic eruption as well.

Other notable things to consider:

- Neptune will be going out of its own rulership, where it has been since 2004 (Neptune entered Pisces in 2011, but before that it was in mutual reception with Uranus in Pisces). Illusions and fantasies are not as likely to be as effective or hold as much sway as they did during that period.

- This will be the first time since 1968 that there have not been at least two outer planets (not counting Pluto) in the "collective" half of the zodiac (Libra-Pisces, and yes, I checked Jupiter and Saturn too), and for a couple of years (until August 2028, when Jupiter reenters Libra), there will be no outer planets in the "collective" half of the zodiac (again, not counting Pluto), something that hasn't happened since at least 1942. Based on this I wouldn't be surprised if the whole notion of a "global order" starts to unravel.

Hi JMG,

Best wishes for this equinoctial season to you and all others who are marking it.

I recently attended a 4-day residential workshop and retreat with some figures in the anthroposophical scene, including one chap (Are Thoresen) who is becoming quite prominent in that world.

I found the man's work intriguing enough to take a punt on the retreat, but I find myself increasingly doubtful about some of the claims he is making. These include:

* What he calls the 'elemental' world, which is a layer between the material and the etheric, has been opening up for about 70 years now.
* This elemental world is apparently 'evil' and needs to be 'christianized', as he puts it, using a method he calls Christ-point healing.
* He is mainly talking about technology, but he has performed this 'healing' on other spirits as well, including an entity he saw in Lithuania that he believed was a demon at the centre of a neo-pagan group. The leader of this neopagan group lost her mind about a week later, and hasn't recovered.
* He appears to conflate 'elementals' with 'demons' all the time, and claims that all disease either creates or is caused by elementals.
* Homeopathic medicines, biodynamic preparations, etc are all losing efficacy with the opening of up of the 'elemental' world.
* The vast majority of medical treatments, whether allopathic or holistic, cause 'translocation' of the disease to other hosts, and should therefore be dropped. His Christ-point healing is one of the only reliable methods to transform disease without translocating it.

This is pretty much it in a nutshell. I previously thought he was an interesting eccentric with some potentially valuable insights, but after having spent 4 days with him I am starting to question whether he is perhaps simply very misled by his visions, and indeed whether he is in fact causing some absolute havoc in the etheric world with his methods.

I would appreciate hearing your own thoughts, JMG, as well as others - particularly those who are homeopaths or other healers. Of course, this is all asked from a purely theoretical and metaphysical standpoint, and should not be construed as seeking any form of medical advice.

comments.title: Spring Eclipses Attracting a Lot of Attention
comments.content:
Not so much a question but a ramble with a request for your opinion, JMG. As some of us are aware, eclipses are important portents and tend to attract a reasonable amount of attention from the astrological community and some excitement from the astronomy geeks. But this time round it seems that attention is being paid by a more diverse set of groups.

Among the vedic astrologers, this is a biggie because the April solar eclipse coincides with the start of the vedic new year (the first new moon in Pisces, according to siderial reckoning). Talk about starting the year on the wrong foot! Anyway, the goings on in the heavens in March and April (lunar eclipse, retrograde Mercury, position of Mars, etc.) are pretty ghastly and kind of dog-pile on the themes of ‘truth getting out there’ and ‘generous helpings of violent confrontations’.

I tend to not pay much attention to Western astrology, but I have picked up from various sources that the Jupiter-Uranus conjunction shortly after the solar eclipse in April is not exactly a harbinger of ‘happy days’.

But what has really caught my eye is the attention that religious communities are giving these eclipses. For Muslims, the solar eclipse happens at the closing of the holy month of Ramadan (but talk about the significations of this are hush-hush due to the current fundamentalist flavour of Islam these days). And for some Jews the planned red heifer sacrifice scheduled for March 30 (between the lunar and solar eclipses) is a big prophetic deal but I personally I am perplexed by the timing, and I am sure that some astrologically oriented Jews may feel the same way (seems to me like ‘forcing the Hand of God’ and thumbing one’s nose at the heavenly host – which tends not to be a particularly beneficial path to tread).

But the biggest thing religious thing that caught my attention regarding the eclipses is the chatter within some Christian groups. They have noted that path of the October 2023 eclipse fell on or near a large number of communities named ‘Salem’ (shortform of ‘Jerusalem' and occurred just days after the latest Israeli-Palestinian conflict that was triggered by an event in Jerusalem) and the April 2024 eclipse will fall on or near 7 communities named ‘Nivineh’. The Bible mentions Nivineh in connection with Johah (as that’s where he was commanded to preach and by doing so got the residents to humble themselves, thereby sparing them from God’s wrath); in addition, the star that will be obscured by the eclipse is called, in Arabic, ‘belly of the whale’. So, they are calling this the ‘Johah eclipse’ and there is a lot of concern that the eclipse will trigger the 3 deadly days of darkness (as per Jonah’s incarceration in the great fish’s belly and as prophesized by many Christian sources). I am not saying that I believe in this, but I make note of it. My spidey senses are telling me that a lot of people of faith are really riled up right now and feel that ‘something’s gotta give’ in the very near future. This frantic energy will need to be grounded - somehow.

I don’t know if you have had any observations or ponderings over the subject lately, but I figure that it is worth enquiring.

Ron M

replies.title: Re: God(dess) of Forests
replies.content:
I think many traditional cultures envisioned a supernatural entity as a God/dess of a *specific* forest rather than of *all* forests collectively. Scandinavian lore speaks of the skogs-ra or forest-ruler, but there are different rulers in different forests. Same with Greek grove-divinities, and grove-divinities of the Germanic tribes as mentioned by Tacitus, and many more.

I think this is part of a larger difference between us and ancient cultures: they often focused more locally whereas we tend to focus more universally, perhaps thanks to Christianity and Western philosophy with their more universal outlooks. The older outlook was very much tied to the physical world and the local interfaces between physical and metaphysical worlds. “A God of all forests” might, to them, have less power and less relevance than the God of *this* forest, right here in your face and watching every move you make!

Winifred Hodge Rose

replies.title: Re: God(dess) of Forests
replies.content:
If you enjoy fictional explorations of Deities and beliefs, I like Michael Scott Rohan's trilogy "The Anvil of Ice," "The Forge in the Forest," and "Hammer of the Sun." The trilogy combines the legend of Volund with Finnish mythology and peri-glacial Hyperborean / Atlantean mythology. The middle book, Forge in the Forest, largely involves an existential conflict between the Volund-character and Finnish forest-deities, and the values that they each symbolize. Whether one agrees with the author's stand in these matters or not, it's an interesting exploration of ideas.

Winifred Hodge Rose

comments.title: Near Death Experiences Research Foundation (NDERF)
comments.content:
Not a question.
This morning I found a site called the Near Death Experiences Research Foundation. Don't know anything about them but below is a snippet from someone whose experiences they describe as:
Among the most profound NDEs ever shared with NDERF. Multiple NDEs age 3-7 as a result of life-threatening abuse by foster parents. Remarkable spiritual insights. The most detailed discussion of life on non-earthly planets in any NDE ever reported to the best of our knowledge.

It is hard to comprehend that humans and other beings associated with earth might have such a role as described in Sandi T's account (below) when humans are so small and seemingly insignificant! Then again, what a weird place this world seems to be. I guess we might find out when the time comes - probably something stranger than we can possibly imagine!

***
4838. Sandi T NDEs 10/18/2020. NDEs 9082
As I rose from my body, I found my friend waiting there for me. My friend was a humanoid-shaped form of light who radiating kindness, love, and patience. We ignored the commotion of the foster mother trying to revive the body. I stood looking up at the body. Everything is 'up' from the perspective of a child, although I could clearly see everything around me.

'What is it that you wish to ask, but have not?' It conveyed the question soundlessly.

'Why?' With that question, I asked a dozen others, 'Why me? Why suffering? Why this horrible world? Why did I go back when I could leave? Why would I come here and accept such horrific things when I am a spark of the divine, a portion of the Great Intelligence?' It was a cry of confusion, anger, pain, and loss.

It held out a proverbial hand and asked me, 'Are you sure you want to know? All that you suffer now will go easier on you if you do not know.'

I pondered, searching myself. Did I want to know if it would bring me more pain? In the end, I decided that I wanted to know. I could tell that my friend already knew my decision. There was a nod, and we were off....

***[jump]

...Everywhere in the universe was great love, dignity, respect, and compassion. It was so exquisite that I can't contain my tears as I have a diminished capacity to remember this experience because this is all that my brain can encompass while in this reduced, small, and limited form. To go from where I was and into this has been almost unbearable. To truly know what lies beyond and to know beyond all doubt that it is magnificent and fantastic beyond all conception, makes living here, in this form, so hard. I try not to think about it. Another reason I have rarely spoken of my experience is that it makes me yearn ever harder to return to it.

After a great deal of time exploring, seeing beautiful and wonderful sights, we stopped in space near a nebula. Nebulas are even more beautiful than they appear in photos.

'That is the answer to your question.'

I understood that everything that we do here on Earth, all that we are, all that we experience, allows creation to exist. Every beautiful thing, every wonderful being and creature, whether on earth or in any universe, relies upon people who are on the extremely rare places like Earth.

The Great Intelligence (god) is a paradox. It is completely loving and fully unlimited. Which by the definition of paradox, means it is impossible? It cannot be limited only to love; it cannot be limited to only being unlimited; or it is not unlimited.

Earth is a place where the unlimited becomes limited; where the singular becomes many. Here, it can know community and loneliness. It can know heartache and hope. It can know all which an unlimited being of pure love cannot. It can conceive and perceive evil; which in truth it cannot do this either. To solve the paradox, it must experience helplessness and limitation and all as it is Real. In this place, it is all so REAL.

So what is free will? Free will is the option to come here to help solve the paradox of 'god'. To be all that we are not, so that everything wondrous and joyful may continue to exist. So that love itself may continue to exist. So that the Unlimited is not limited to being only unlimited.

Why are the answers always, 'simply to exist' and 'to choose love' and 'to learn how to love'? Because all you need to do, to solve the paradox, is to exist. And as we exist here, each time we choose love, we expand the universe. Love is life's longing for itself. Despite the reality of what we live, even the darkest souls among us cannot help but to reach, to yearn, and move towards goodness and towards love.

For love is the true nature of who we are. And when we experience horrible things, the question 'why' comes to mind because it is the central question of love, life, and of this world. The answer is 'so that all things might continue to exist.'

Every soul chose to come here and to suffer because of love. Each soul loves the universe, loves life, and loves this world and ALL of the worlds. Each soul loves ALL of the people so immensely and intensely that they chose to come here so that all the universes may teem with beautiful, joyful LIFE.

Every creature that I saw, acknowledges that your life gives them the gift of life. And when each soul goes 'home' after they die, they will know the rewards of their own gift, too. The 'reward' for their sacrifice will be joy, love, and feeling incredible, wonderful, beautiful joy at the LIFE and the LOVE everywhere in the universe.

When you go home, you meet your own soul. You willingly came here to forget yourself. You willingly came here to save every beautiful and wonderful thing. By suffering what 'god' cannot, you give the gift of life.
***
Full account:
https://www.nderf.org/Experiences/1sandi_t_ndes.html

Hello,

Q1. Have you or anybody you know ever worked with Franz Bardon's material? If so, have you/they had good results with it?

Q2. I've heard you briefly say (in print and on podcasts) that magic can transform your life. Is that really true? If so, what do you think a beginner could do in a 3-5 year period?

I know that this is a complicated issue. It depends on the desired changes, experience of the operator, the type of magic being used etc.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Good heavens, yes. I know several dozen serious practitioners of Bardon's system, who have had very good results with it. It's an effective system -- the one issue is that you have to do each step very thoroughly before proceeding to the next. If you skip anything, or go over it lightly, you can run into trouble.

2) That's entirely true -- but there's a catch. It doesn't mean that you can make your life whatever you want it to be. If you don't have any musical talent, for example, magic is not going to make you a rock star. It's also not going to make you filthy rich -- if you want to get rich, become a stockbroker, not a mage.

What magic can do is quite simple. It can help you clear away all the self-imposed obstacles that keep you from being able to discover and use the talents that you do have, and it can teach you how to stop other people from preventing you from being what you can be. That's not a fast process, and -- crucially -- it's not a painless one. You'll be putting in half an hour or so each day of practices, some of which will be more boring and frustrating than anything else you've ever done in your life. You'll also get to wake up to the ways in which you're frustrating yourself -- and this can be a bitter, humiliating experience. (Trust me, I've been there.) The payoffs are worth the discomfort, but you have to put up with the discomfort first.

I've written about some of this in my book Mystery Teachings from the Living Earth. One of these days I'll be writing a more general book on the purposes and results of magic, but that's for the future.

comments.content:
In my natal chart Saturn (retrograde) is in Aries and in my 10th House.

From what I've found online Saturn in the 10th is viewed along the lines of "Saturn creates ambitious people with great range of knowledge, excellent organizational abilities and a desire for power.." etc. Parker's astrology states that "the individual may have to shoulder a lot of responsibility".

Reason for the post is that someone whose opinion matters to me has stated "This is one of the loudest warning sirens in astrology. In a natal chart, it predicts a sudden and final fall from a position of power..." Oh boy.

Over the past few years I have backed away from trying to advance in my career and just maintain. I have been successful in moving from a supervisory to "individual contributor" role. I'm also trying get out from under my organizational egregor but have no clear path at this time.

Any natal astrologers that don't mind providing their thoughts on the limited info provided please feel free. If anyone has the time I can also add the various additional aspects Saturn has and it's relation and position of the Aries sign ruler Mars.

Thank you

replies.title: Re: Opinion on Saturn placement natal chart
replies.content: I'll leave this for natal astrologers, since that's not a branch of astrology I practice professionally.

replies.title: Re: Opinion on Saturn placement natal chart
replies.content:
I've had jobs where I worked with job descriptions. And I know some astrology. I would think of it this way.

Saturn = structure, limitation, organization, boundaries, achievement through discipline or risk of failure through lack of self-discipline
Aries = initiation, new beginning, self-determination, the head, the springtime
10th house = organized effectiveness in society, such as business and career
Retrograde = the relevant factors can be challenging because they seem to be going backward. Extra focus, awareness, and action necessary to catch up and move ahead in this area of life

To me, this does not prove a negative fate about using power for self-directed career success or as the head of an organization. It doesn't mean you would inevitably have a big fall if you're the one who sets the direction, prioritizes teamwork, guides the rules.

It does suggest that if you want to succeed at these things, you might find it harder than average to do so. You would need to work harder than the typical big boss. Your schemes could face more challenge and risk than if other people tried to do the same thing. Not necessarily that you'll fail! Only that the price of success would be higher for you. Your advisor was giving you a worst-case scenario based on your chart, not a prophecy.

You might be happier and more easily succeed in a situation where you aren't required to be the self-starter, self-organized Big Boss of New Teamwork. Individual Contributor, not manager, would fit this.

Now, to find a more easygoing organization where people get the job done but don't Live to Succeed. Some companies are known for quality of life, not aggressive ambition.

Or, you could be a freelancer where any one client's bureaucracy can't suck you in to their egregor. It may be that you could eventually have clients who are very happy for your unpretentious approach, who like that you aren't ambitious for their jobs but only to help them succeed. This might be worth more money in the future!

I would suggest the books of Herman Holtz and Alan Weiss on a low-stress consulting lifestyle. Holtz mentioned that he got a cheap office with a fireplace so he could feel cozy while he works. Weiss mentioned that he didn't mind having calls from his clients at any time, because he worked from home and could also take his dogs with him to the post office during the work day.

You might redefine how valuable an ordinary worker bee could really be!

replies.title: Re: Opinion on Saturn placement natal chart
replies.content:

Fortunately, it wasn't an advisor giving a worst-case scenario. I stumbled upon the the comment that in a natal chart the placement predicts a sudden and final fall from power in a blog on mundane astrology of all places.

Your framing of the issue is helpful and will help my thoughts going forward.

On the positive side, the garden exploded with bounty and the entire area around it became exceptionally lush. The negative side was that the garden and environs developed a menacing, unfriendly energy—hostile.

Possibly unrelated, but his time there was short—only one summer. He was gay and developed AIDS, and left the commune for treatment. Since this was the 1980s, he sadly died quickly after that.

As I noted to another commenter, two billion people are carrying cell phone cameras with them all the time now, and yet the rate of UFO photos has declined steadily; so has the rate of abduction reports; so are all the other markers of pseudo-extraterrestrial activity. replies.title: Re: Fairy rings

replies.title: Re: Fairy rings

In the usual Plutonian fashion, the UFO phenomenon came in with a bang and is fading out with a whimper; it seemed to promise the total transformation of human existence, and then never followed through; it became a dumping ground for a giddy assortment of cravings and fantasies; it split in classic Plutonian fashion into utopian and apocalyptic subsets; and it created an obsessive subculture that will never admit that the great whatchamacallit that will change everything is never going to happen. Space travel, nuclear power, Communism -- there's a long list of such things, and the UFO phenomenon is another of them.

replies.title: Re: Fairy rings
replies.content:
I'd gotten the impression by osmosis that a tension between utopian and apocalyptic expectations was supposed to be a Piscean thing, not a Plutonian one. Is the idea that a Plutonian influence results in the the utopian and apocalyptic camps breaking contact with each other? Does that kind of schism require a Piscean state of imagination to start from? Would a Piscean-influenced phenomenon look more like an attempt to keep a grip on the potential hidden unity of the perspectives?

I do have a question I've been meaning to ask about the relation of human effort to the prevention of Plutonian-associated apocalyptic outcomes like global nuclear war. Like, does the influence of Pluto mean that a non-catastrophe is nearly guaranteed even in the absence of anyone bestirring themselves to prevent it? (Or is the meaning of Pluto more subtle, so that it also covers the visible effects of real game-theoretic threats that should have been too "unthinkable" to enact, the same way it covers the visible effects of real subconscious motivations?) But it seems too late in the day to get very far into that right now. Also, you did say "nuclear power" and not "nuclear weapons". So maybe you only meant to specify that the Plutonian thing that could have been predicted in advance as almost certainly never going to happen was apocalyptic levels of nuclear power plant meltdowns, not apocalyptic levels of nuclear bombing.

replies.title: Re: Fairy rings
replies.content:
I don't think it's accidental that Pluto took on temporary planet status during the immediate aftermath of the age of Pisces. (Think back on all the wild astrological prophecies made because Pluto was doing this or that; how many of them actually happened?) But you're right that in a Plutonian situation, the two sides of the utopia-or-oblivion delusion cut the cord that unites them and go zooming off on their own trajectories.

As for your question, planets shape and direct human behavior as much as they do anything else; human beings aren't insulated from astrological forces, after all! Yes, I mentioned nuclear power, because that's so good an example of the paired but detached fantasies of utopia and mass death, but nuclear weapons are also highly Plutonian. I've written at length about them here:

https://thearchdruidreport-archive.200605.xyz/2012/11/deterrence-in-age-of-decline.html

- Christopher from California

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Ouch! Well, a few other thoughts:

1) In the 18th to early 19th century (maybe before? I know about it from revolutionary-era America), it was common for folks to wake up for a while in the middle of the night. They'd light a candle and read, or make love, or other in-house activities, and then go back to sleep. Of course, they also mostly went to bed at dusk or not long after. Maybe you're wired for something like that?

2) The other thing folks will swear by in improving sleep is "sleep hygiene," which means having an environment conducive to sleep - dark, slightly cool, no stressful noises. The easiest way to get most of the way there is with an eye mask and ear plugs, but some folks swear by white noise machines or the like. Temperature appears to be highly individualized, but most folks all asleep more easily if they're a bit too cool rather than a bit too warm. Experimenting with different combinations of bedding and pajamas might help.

3) On the light thing specifically, light appears to be one of the main ways our body sets our sleep/wake cycle, and not just while actively trying to sleep. Some folks say getting bright, sun-colored light as soon after waking as possible is good, others advocate for tooling around in partial light while you gradually wake up. Eye color may factor in to this (I have blue eyes and am very sensitive to bright light in the morning, my wife has brown and can't see with the level of light I turn on first thing in the morning).

The other big culprit here these days is obviously screens. Screens are bright, often rather blue-tinted (which is closer to sunlight than fire/candlelight), and the things we're looking at them tend to amp us up (news, social media, etc). Putting some kind of after-dark light-shifter app on your phone/computer can be good (f.lux is one of the originals, and it's open source and free, but Apple products have "night shift" built in), but even better to make a rule to start avoiding screens as much as possible some amount of time before bed/after dark (obviously, the more screenless time before bed, the better).

Anyhow, not being able to sleep truly sucks, I wish you luck in finding a way to get better rest more consistently.

Cheers,
Jeff

comments.title: Transplants
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

Thank you for hosting Magic Mondays. It has truly been a great help!

I find myself in a strange scenario. A while ago, a person close to me was was diagnosed with a rare type of leukemia. The best option for treatment involved a procedure where a person could donate their stem cells and, in effect, replace the recipient's blood with their own. They found that I was compatible, so we went through the rounds and after a few painful months, it went successfully!

However, I've noticed strange things. My energy and motivation, which in the past was variable, has become more consistent but generally lower. Like the peaks and valleys of the Rockies vs the Appalachians. I also noticed some subtle shifts in the recipient's personality. Quicker to laugh, quicker to anger, and generally more active among other things. It's not like they've transformed into a different person. More so, it feels like they've picked up on some of my quirks (the good and the bad). I cannot shake the feeling that there is a link between us.

After all this happened, I started learning about magic and I am wondering about the unintended effects. What do the occult traditions have to say about transplants? What effect, beyond the material plane, can/do they have? How might this effect a) my spiritual and magic practice in the future and b) the recipient if I continue?

replies.title: Re: Transplants
replies.content:
You've replaced the other person's life force with part of your own, and that's going to have a significant effect. Yes, there is a link between you, and it will last as long as both of you are alive. That said, you basically made a sacrifice of part of your life force to save the other person's life; that's a very good thing to do, and you'll benefit in this life or another from the truckload of positive karma that's earned you.

I recommend, if you're not doing this already, doing daily breathing and relaxation exercises -- that will help you recharge your etheric body and rebuild your energy. Since stem cell transplants didn't exist until recently, nobody knows the answers to your two last questions, but it's unlikely to be a problem -- again, for karmic reasons.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The beauty of Placidus (and other unequal house systems, such as Regiomontanus and Koch) is that they allow this kind of flexibility. The sign on the cusp tells you which planet rules each house; in the example you use, your 5th is ruled by Uranus. Venus, however, is exalted in Pisces, and so you have an exalted Venus in the 5th. Each of those tells you something about how 5th house matters tend to play out in your life.
